coredata.dc:title: High yield glucose assisted carbonization of soy flour for dye removal applications
coredata.dc:creator: Choudhary, Raveena
Pandey, O.P.
Brar, Loveleen K.
coredata.dc:description: Suspension of glucose and soy flour (in fixed weight ratio of 1:0.3) was hydrothermally treated to synthesize N-doped carbons. A systematic study has been carried out to understand the role of glucose in inducing soy flour carbonization. The formation mechanism of N-doped carbon spheroids from high protein agro waste and sachharides has been explained on the basis of structural, morphological and chemical chracterizations in conjunction with available literature. In the beginning carbon quantum dots nucleate from the sachharide. The carbonized N-containing graphene/aromatized flakes formed by carbonization of high protein soy flour and glucose curve around this carbon quantum dot nucleus to form spheroids. On the basis of high yield and preliminary results for dye adsorption, GS1 sample (w/v:: 2.15g/15 mL), was selected for the further studies. GS1 sample was tested for its adsorption efficiency of cationic (MB and CV) as well as anionic (EBT and MO) dyes. The influence of adsorbate pH and adsorbate concentration on the adsorption efficiency of the synthesized sample is studied. The maximum dye adsorbed at equilibrium is 52 mg/g and 31.5 mg/g for MB and EBT respectively. A comparison with literature shows that our samples are comparable and many a times better than the prevelant adsorbents. Adsorption isotherms and reaction kinetics (at constant temperature and adsorbent concentration) are analyzed to determine the adsorption mechanisms for anionic as well as cationic dyes. Further as a proof of concept, photocatalytic studies of dyes were carried out and it was observed that % decolorization is enhanced in the presence of UV/Visible/Sunlight indicating the capability of the samples for photocatalytic dye degradation.

head.ce:title: High yield glucose assisted carbonization of soy flour for dye removal applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Materials Science, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology, Patiala, 147004, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Materials Science
Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Materials Science, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology, Patiala, 147004, Punjab, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Suspension of glucose and soy flour (in fixed weight ratio of 1:0.3) was hydrothermally treated to synthesize N-doped carbons. A systematic study has been carried out to understand the role of glucose in inducing soy flour carbonization. The formation mechanism of N-doped carbon spheroids from high protein agro waste and sachharides has been explained on the basis of structural, morphological and chemical chracterizations in conjunction with available literature. In the beginning carbon quantum dots nucleate from the sachharide. The carbonized N-containing graphene/aromatized flakes formed by carbonization of high protein soy flour and glucose curve around this carbon quantum dot nucleus to form spheroids. On the basis of high yield and preliminary results for dye adsorption, GS1 sample (w/v:: 2.15g/15 mL), was selected for the further studies. GS1 sample was tested for its adsorption efficiency of cationic (MB and CV) as well as anionic (EBT and MO) dyes. The influence of adsorbate pH and adsorbate concentration on the adsorption efficiency of the synthesized sample is studied. The maximum dye adsorbed at equilibrium is 52 mg/g and 31.5 mg/g for MB and EBT respectively. A comparison with literature shows that our samples are comparable and many a times better than the prevelant adsorbents. Adsorption isotherms and reaction kinetics (at constant temperature and adsorbent concentration) are analyzed to determine the adsorption mechanisms for anionic as well as cationic dyes. Further as a proof of concept, photocatalytic studies of dyes were carried out and it was observed that % decolorization is enhanced in the presence of UV/Visible/Sunlight indicating the capability of the samples for photocatalytic dye degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: A major concern for achieving sustainable future is the large quantities of pollutants such as pesticides, pharmaceutical wastes, phenolic compounds, dyes etc. which get released into the water resources and soil on daily basis. The removal of these pollutants from waste water requires specialized teritary water purification methods [1–4]. Dyes are an integral part of paper/pulp, textile/printing, plastics, cosmetics and food industry today. Most of the currently used dyes are synthetic and are non-biodegradable. Untreated effluents lead to mixing of these dyes with water bodies. There they pose grave danger to human and aquatic life by reducing the amount of dissolved oxygen in water, changing the spectrum of sunlight reaching inside the water bodies and also due to their mutagenic and carcinogenic nature [5,6]. Numerous chemical, physical and biological treatment techniques involving precipitation, reverse osmosis, coagulation and flocculation, ozonation, electrochemical techniques and adsorption are available for removal of these pollutants [4,7–11]. Among these, adsorption is the most widely used technique due to its simple implementation and cost effectiveness. Adsorption is a surface phenomenon in which pollutants get attached to the surface of an adsorbent via either physical or chemical bonding [12]. Different materials such as clay, zeolite, silica gel, bio char, etc. have been used extensively for the removal of different pollutants from water via adsorption. The process of adsorption is affected by the pH of adsorbate, concentration of adsorbent and adsorbate, temperature of adsorbate, adsorption time etc. [13]. Porosity and chemical groups present on the adsorbate surface are key parameters in determining the nature and kinetics of the adsorption process. The chemical nature of the surface affects the acidic/basic nature of adsorbent, interaction with specific compounds and its hydrophobicity [14]. Carbon based materials find intensive applications as solid adsorbents in contrast with other materials due to their low cost and easy processing, high surface area, surface porosity, tunable surface chemistry, excellent stability, ease of regeneration etc. Many carbon based adsorbents are available for waste water treatment that include graphene, CNTs, carbon nanofibres, nanodiamonds, nanoporous activated carbon, graphitic C3N4, graphene oxide composites [15–18]. Among these, porous activated carbons have attracted high attention due to their fast adsorption rate, chemical selectivity as well as high adsorption capacity [19,20].

Nitrogen (N-) doping of carbon adsorbates enhances the basic character of porous carbons as N-doping sites act as lewis basic sites. This results in enhanced adsorption capacity as well as selectivity [21]. Sánchez-Sánchez et al. [22]. reported that N-doping in carbon adsorbent results in an enhanced adsorption capacity for cationic as well as anionic dyes. Inspite of the well documented enhancement of the dye adsorption capacity by N-doped carbon adsorbates, it is still a challenge to dope nitrogen by green and clean methods using simple and abundant precursors. N-doping is usually done by using melamine [23], ammonia [24], ethylene diamine [25] and thiourea [26]. Some bio based precursors with high protein content such as hair, cocoon silk etc., have also been used for effective nitrogen doping [27,28]. The proteins in the precursor can act as the source for both nitrogen and carbons. Agro wastes rich in proteins can emerge as the cheapest source to synthesize N-doped carbons. Defatted soy meal contains 50.5% proteins, 34.2% carbohydrates and 15.3% fiber, ash and fat [29]. He et al. [30]. reported that the standard chemical composition of defatted soy meal is crude protein, dietary fibre, amino acids and carbohydrate which makes soy flour a good source of carbon and nitrogen. Alatalo et al. [31]. reported the use of soy flour as a nitrogen source for N-doping in activated carbons prepared using glucose. Various methods have been used for the preparation of N-doped carbons such as chemical vapor deposition [32], pyrolysis [33], solvothermal [34], hydrothermal [35] etc. Among all these methods we found hydrothermal method to be most suitable because it is a low temperature green process.

The present study focuses on the synthesis of N-doped carbons via hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) method using defatted soy flour. Glucose has been added to induce the carbonization rather than hydrochar/biochar generally resulting from the hydrothermal treatment of proteins/protein rich materials. Combination of glucose and soy flour in the initial mixture results in N-doped carbon spheres having high yield, larger size and higher affinity for dye adsorption when compared with the soy flour bio char and glucose derived carbon spheres. The as synthesized N-doped carbons have been used for the treatment of waste water containing cationic and anionic dyes by adsorption. The dye adsorbed at equilibrium for 1 mg/L dye concentration is 25.9 mg/g and 3.07 mg/g for MB and EBT respectively. A comparison with literature shows that our samples are comparable and many times better than the prevelant adsorbents. The kinetic modeling and isotherm analysis of the adsorption results has also been undertaken to determine the adsorption rate and adsorption mechanism. Based on our earlier results [36], the synthesized samples have also been tested for the photocatalytic degradation of the dyes. We have used agrowaste for the synthesis of N-doped carbons in single low temperature step giving rise to high yield of spheroidal adsorbents exhibiting high adsorption capacity and easy handling capabilities for applications in industrial waste water treatment.

In a typical experiment, required amount of glucose and soy flour in 1:0.3 w/w ratio was dispersed in 15 mL deionized water (resistivity- 18.2 MΩ-cm, Millipore Q3 system) by magnetic stirring. The solution was then transferred to a teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in an oven which was heated to and maintained at 180 °C for 8 h. The resulting brown colored precipitates were separated by centrifuge and washed several times with deionized water and dried at 80 °C in a hot air oven for 24 h. Different batches of samples were prepared by varying the total precursor weight in the same quantity of water volume. In another set of experiments, to understand the role of simultaneous addition of soy flour and glucose, samples with only soy flour and glucose were also synthesized with the same total weight of the precursor (2.15 g). The details of the synthesized samples are given in Table 1 .

The morphology of the prepared samples was studied using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JSM-6510 at 15 kV), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) (JEOL 2100 F, operating at 200 kV). Elemental analysis of the samples was carried out using the electron dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) using the Oxford INCAx-act detector attached with the SEM. The size of the synthesized carbons was determined using Axio-vision software and analyzed using Origin™ Software. Crystalline phases of the prepared samples were studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (PANalytical Xpert-Pro diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (1.5406 Å; Ni filter) in the range 5–90° (2θ, step size = 0.013°). Microtrac Bell was used to obtain N2 adsorption-desorption behavior of the samples with pretreatment at 150 °C. To determine the surface area and pore size distribution, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods were used, respectively. Chemical bonds present in the samples were investigated using Fourier transmission infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Agilent Cary 660, from 400 to 4000 cm−1). To determine reflectance/absorbance behavior, UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi U–3900H) was used in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Surface chemistry of the synthesized samples was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PHI 5000 Versa Prob II, FEI Inc. with the help of Al Kα radiation (1486.7 eV, Ag standard)). To determine the point of zero charge (pHpzc), the salt addition method was used. In a typical experiment, 10 mL NaCl solution (0.005 M) having a pH value from 4 to 11 (adjusted by using HCl and NaOH) was prepared. 3 mg of the sample was added to this solution and agitated for 24 h by magnetic stirring. The final pH of the solution was measured and change in pH was calculated. The plot between the initial pH and change in pH is used to determine pHpzc for the given sample.

To understand the performance of the prepared samples for dye removal from wastewater, the following dyes were used: methylene blue (MB), crystal violet (CV), methyl orange (MO) and eriochrome black-T (EBT). First two are cationic dyes and the latter are anionic dyes. Adsorption studies were carried out in 50 mL dye solution (adsorbate) of required concentration (typically 1 mg/L) containing adsorbent (typically 20 mg/L) under continuous stirring in dark conditions. After a specific time period, 3 mL of the solution was removed, adsorbents were separated by centrifugation and the dye concentration in solution was determined using UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The adsorption capacity of the sample has also been studied by varying the adsorbate solution pH from 2 to 10 using 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH solution. To study the adsorption isotherms and kinetics, the experiments were performed by varying the adsorbate concentration.

To check the photodegradation efficiency of the sample, the batch experiments were performed on MB, CV and EBT dyes in the similar manner as for the adsorption study. The experiments were conducted in the presence of UV light (Philips, 125 W; Hg lamp; λem = 250–400 nm; intensity = ~14000 lx), visible light (Philips household CFL; 85W; intensity = ~8900 lx) and also sunlight (intensity = ~17500 lx).

Fig. 1 gives the representative SEM images of the synthesized samples. SEM micrographs indicate that both precursors (glucose and soy flour) combined together under the HTC conditions to form spheroids of different sizes. In certain areas complete spherical features are observed, whereas in other areas flattened curve surface, an indication of beginning of formation of spheroids was observed. The spheroids have smooth surfaces.

Fig. 2 a gives the size distribution profile of spheroids for sample GS1 and is representative for all the samples. The data was fitted to lognormal profile and the calculated spheroid size for all the samples is mentioned in Table 1. SEM images and the analyzed data clearly show that the spheroid size of the synthesized samples decreases with decreasing precursor concentration in the initial dispersion. The variation of size with concentration is shown in Fig. 2b. SEM micrographs of samples synthesized using only soy flour ( S ) and glucose ( G ) samples (Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b) clearly shows that the hydrothermal treatment of soy alone does not lead to self assembled structures/spheroids. Also the presence of soy flour in the initial dispersion has resulted in larger sized spheres with non uniform size distribution as compared with when only glucose is used. Another important aspect to emerge from the experiments was that the yield for the GS1 sample (600 mg) is much higher as compared with samples S (350 mg) and G (13 mg) with the starting precursor weight of 2.15 g. In general the yield from a HTC experiment depends on amount of precursor and the reaction. Thus combining the glucose (sachharide) and high protein precursor such as soy flour results in enhanced carbonization and high yield. The elemental analysis of GS1 , S and G samples using EDS is shown in Fig. S1. It can be seen that sample G carries peak of only carbon and oxygen, while, GS1 and S have carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The results of the EDS analysis are given in Table S1.

The structural nature of the synthesized material was investigated using XRD. The XRD patterns for GS1 sample (Fig. 4 a) show a broad hump at 2θ ≈ 21°, and a peak at 2θ ≈ 26°. The hump at 21° is a signature of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and attributed to (002) lattice spacing of amorphous carbon material (indexed as (002)-CQD) [37]. For undoped carbon nanospheres (Sample G ) the XRD line profile shows a hump at 2θ ≈ 24° and corresponded with (002) plane of graphitic carbon (indexed as (002)-CNS) [38]. The XRD line profile for the S Sample revealed prominent peaks at 2θ ≈ 22° and 26°. The peak of at 22° is attributed to the (002) plane in amorphous carbon having oxygen containing functional group (indexed as (002)-AO) [39]. The peak of 26° is attributed to (002) plane of graphitic carbon (indexed as (002)-g). Both these peaks confirm the biochar nature of the sample S . Presence of the peak at 26° in the GS1 sample indicates the presence of graphitic carbon in the sample along with the CQDs. This XRD data needs to be explained in context with the SEM micrographs of spheroids obtained for GS1 .

Absorbance spectra from the UV–Visible spectroscopy measurement for the GS1 , S and G samples are shown in Fig. 4b. It was observed that the main absorbance peak of S sample appears at nearly 200 nm in the UV region, which shows the π-π* transition between the bands of graphitic carbon material. Two main absorbance peaks of G sample at nearly 280 nm and 224 nm indicated the n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC bond [40]. GS1 sample shows an adsorption peak near 250 nm, clearly indicating the π-π* transition between the domains of graphitic carbon and this matches with data obtained for CQDs in literature [41]. This data supports the presence of broad peak in XRD line profile at ~21° for GS1 rather than at 22° (for S ) or 24° (for G ). The low intensity peak/hump near 280 nm indicates the lowered conc. of CO bonds in the sample. This indicated that the CQDs are not exposed to oxygen rich environs after formation. Inset of Fig. 4b gives the Tauc's plot for GS1 sample. The band gaps for G , S and GS1 to GS6 samples were determined. The band gap increased from 4.5 to 5 eV with decrease in the size of the sample from GS1 to GS6 (Table 1).

SEM micrographs of GS1 sample show large spheroids of average size 4.96 μm. This data is in contrast with the XRD line profile and UV–Vis data which indicates the presence of CQDs in the sample. It can be conjectured that the CQDs will not be visible in the magnifications of SEM. GS1 samples are separated at the centrifuge speed of 4500 rpm. CQDs will not separate out of the solution at such low speeds. To understand the internal morphology of the carbon spheroids and the TEM/HR-TEM studies were carried out. To ensure that the clear picture emerges of the internal structure the GS6 sample with average size of 350 nm was used for these studies. Fig. 5 a gives the representative TEM image for the GS6 sample 'clearly showing the nonuniform size distribution of solid spheroids. The non spheroidal material (also seen in SEM micrographs) under HR-TEM analysis emerged as graphitic carbon with interplaner spacing of ~0.33 nm, corresponding to 2θ ≈ 26° (Fig. 5b) [42]. HR-TEM of the spheroids shows that the core of the spheroids consists of CQDs with interplanar spacing of 0.41 nm corresponding to 2θ ≈ 21° (Fig. 5c) [43]. These results have been used to predict the synthesis mechanism for the soy-glucose based spheroids under HTC conditions further in the manuscript (Section 3.2).

Fig. 6 a shows the FTIR spectra for the GS1 sample and is representative for all the samples. The characteristic band of O–H is available around 3380 cm−1. Various bonds of carbon with carbon, oxygen, hydrogen are visible and have been marked. Low intensity peak around 1200 and 1500 cm−1 depicts the presence of C–N and CN bond confirming the presence of nitrogen in the sample [25].

Fig. 6b shows the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm and the pore size distribution (inset) for GS1 sample. The BET isotherm displays type II IUPAC curve [44]. The H4 type adsorption hysteresis of the BET data indicated that the sample contains both micropores and mesopores. The calculated BET surface area was 7.43 m2/g. Pore size distribution graph shows that pore size distribution is not uniform for the sample ranging from micropore to mesopore region with a the total calculated pore volume of 0.0393 cm3/g. The pore size varies from 1.5 to 25 nm in mesopore region with a mean pore diameter of 21.1 nm. An important aspect of this data is that the microporous pores (pore size < 2 nm) are substantial in number.

Comparison of XPS survey spectra of GS1 , S and G samples is shown in Fig. 7 a, with peaks at ~284.0 eV, 400.0 eV and 531.0 eV for C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [23]. Absence of N1s peak for G sample clearly indicates that the incorporation of N in the synthesized samples occurred due to presence of soy flour in the initial precursor.

The HR spectra of C1s for GS1 sample deconvoluted into 3 peaks from 284 to 287 eV showing the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, C–N and C–O bonds (Fig. 7b) [45]. The deconvolution of HR spectra of N1s for GS1 sample indicated the presence of pyrrolic and graphitic N as indicated from peaks at 399.55 and 401.56 eV (Fig. 7c) [46,47]. Fig. 7d shows the N1s spectra of S sample and shows the presence of pyrrolic, oxidized and pyridinic nitrogen. From O1s HR spectra for GS1 sample, we can see that the CO bonds are present along with the C–O bonds (Fig. 7e). Tables 2 and 3
give the parameters obtained from XPS analysis indicating the presence of 2.2% predominantly pyrrolic nitrogen in our samples. Different types of N bonding are shown in Fig. 7f.

The % amount of nitrogen present in the soy flour is about 8% [30]. For GS1 sample, the amount of soy flour is ~25% of the total precursor content. XPS results shows that 2.2% nitrogen is present in the sample, which is nearly 2% (25% of 8%). Thus, almost all the nitrogen present in soy flour is getting doped in our samples. This also provides an opportunity for the amount of N-doping to be varied by varying the amount of soy flour in the precursor.
The valence band spectra from the XPS data and band diagram obtained for GS1 sample in conjunction with the UV data are shown in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism of N-doped carbon spheres from glucose and soy flour
ce:section.ce:para: The formation mechanism of the spherical shaped carbons using saccharides via HTC is well documented [39,48]. Under hydrothermal conditions the saccharides undergo dehydration and fragmentation to form phenols, aldehydes and furfural like molecules. These water soluble molecules further polymerize via condensation reactions resulting in aromatization of these polymeric chains through dehydration to form graphene flakes at temperatures greater than 160 °C. These graphene flakes act as nuclei and curves around each other during carbonization to form spherical carbons [39,49].

For the protein rich biomaterials, under the HTC conditions the lower content of hydrocarbons in comparison with proteins results in formation of hydrochar/biochar [49]. The small fraction of carbohydrates present undergoes series of reactions such as hydrolysis, dehydration, polymerization and aggregation to form coke. Simultaneously, the excess amount of proteins undergo hydrolysis to form amino acids and which undergo the maillard reaction with the degradation products of carbohydrates to form N containing ring compounds. These compounds finally combine with the coke to form hydrochar of undetermined structure. The complexity of reaction mechanism followed by proteins hinders the nucleation, and the lack of available nucleation sites prevent the formation of the spherical structures [50–52].

In the present work, glucose has been added in addition to soy flour as a precursor to provide nucleation sites and assist in carbonization. When we take in to consideration the results of various characterizations (XRD, UV–Vis, TEM, HR TEM, SEM) for the N-doped spheroids we arrive at the following formation mechanism (Fig. 8 ): The glucose molecules undergo simultaneous dehydration-hydrolysis and then polymerize to form long chains. This is followed by aromatization leading to formation of pentagonal, hexagonal and heptagonal graphitic rings. Here, the partial carbonization leads to nucleation of CQDs. The formation of CQDs and their presence in the core of the carbon spheres is confirmed by UV–Visible spectroscopy, XRD and HR-TEM of GS1 sample. These carbon quantum dots provide the nucleation sites for the spherical structures to be formed around them. The carbonized soy flour with N contained ring compounds and aromatized glucose curves around the nucleus to form spheres. Due to involvement of bigger polymeric chains of soy flour, the size of the spheres formed is multiple times more than that of G sample spheres [31,51].

The synthesis mechanism discussed here brings together the characterization results for the synthesized samples and the available literature in the field of high protein carbonization [49]. Together they confirm for the role of carbon rich sachharide (glucose) in providing the nucleation sites in the form of CQDs which facilitate the carbonization of the protein and spheroid formation is confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point of zero charge (pHpzc)and its role in determining adsorption characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Surface charge plays a decisive role in determing the adsorption properties of a given material. Point of zero charge (pHpzc) is the value of pH at which the net charge on the surface of the material is zero and is used to determine the surface charge characteristics of the given sample. Fig. 9 gives salt addition method analysis for the GS1 , S and G samples with the pHpzc values coming out to be 4.6, 3.6 and 2.13, respectively (Table 2). Larger pHpzc value for sample S in comparison with sample G is due to the enhanced basicity of the surface as a result of nitrogen presence. The highest pHpzc value at 4.6 is obtained for GS1 inspite of it having lower amount of nitrogen as compared with the S sample. This is because the nitrogen in S sample is partially oxidized as confirmed by N1s XPS spectra of sample S [53]. Hence, GS1 sample is more suitable to adsorb both cationic and anionic dyes at natural water conditions. While the other two samples will more likely to adsorb cationic dyes since above the pHpzc value the surface has a net negative charge.

The pHpzc value for the GS1 sample is 4.6 so initially the adsorption of cationic dyes, MB and CV, from the aqueous solutions was examined. Fig. 10 a shows the evolution of MB dye adsorption onto sample GS1 with time. After 3 hours, the amount of MB and CV adsorbed on the surface of sample GS1 are 51.5% and 50%, respectively. Further, the adsorption studies in dark were also undertaken for anionic dyes EBT and MO. The % adsorption significantly decreases to 6.1% and 0%, for EBT and MO respectively (data not shown).

The adsorption efficiency of GS1 to GS6 samples has been studied for the adsorption of MB dye. It can be seen from Fig. 10b (data was taken for 1 mg/L of MB adsorbed onto 4 mg/L of synthesized sample) that not much variation in the adsorption efficiency of the material with size variation occurs. Since, the yield obtained for GS1 sample is nearly double even when compared with GS2 sample, so, only GS1 sample has been used for further studies.

Fig. 11 a compares the adsorption properties in dark (for MB dye) of GS1 sample with the S and G samples. The results clearly show that inspite of the larger size (4.96 μm) the adsorption properties of the GS1 sample (51%) are superior in comparison to the undoped G sample (640 nm, 38%). Also inspite of the presence of lesser amount of nitrogen as compared with the S sample the GS1 shows better adsorption. We believe this may be due to partial oxidation of the nitrogen in the S sample (as indicated by the XPS results) which modifies the surface charge and hence the adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on adsorption of cationic and anionic dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of all the dye solutions (at the considered concentrations) lies between 5 and 6. The effect of pH on adsorption of cationic as well as anionic dyes was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 11b. At low pH the adsorption of the cationic dye onto the samples is hindered due to more positive charge on the surface of the carbon spheres. With increasing pH, the surface of carbon spheres becomes more and more negative and due to electrostatic attraction and/or hydrogen bonding, uptake of cationic dyes increases on the surface of synthesized sample [54]. It can be observed from Fig. 11b, that the % adsorption of the cationic dyes (MB and CV) reaches the value of 92.2% (MB) and 84.9% (CV) at pH 10. The effect of pH on adsorption was reversed for anionic dyes (EBT and MO). It's clear from Fig. 11b that for EBT, adsorption of about 98% took place at pH 2.4 but with an increase in pH, there is a decrease in the adsorption and it reaches a value of 6% at pH 6.5. In case of MO, the maximum adsorption (8.6%) occurs at pH 2.5, which decreases with increasing pH and becomes zero at pH 4.5 and remains zero with further increase in the pH. The adsorption results are in line with the pHpzc value of 4.5 for GS1 . Above this pH the cationic dyes are adsorbed more and below it the adsorption of anionic dyes increases.

The GS1 was also checked for reusability. This was done by regenerating the sample by centrifuging and washing with water. The regenerated sample was used for further adsorption to check the recyclability of the sample for 14 consecutive cycles for MB dye (100 mg/L of GS1 sample). The small amount of decrease (~6%) in % adsorption observed with 14 cycles and can be attributed to loss of sample during washing (Fig. 12 ). Thus the sample is reusable for dye adsorption applications. Here it is important to note that since GS1 sample consisted of large spheroids so it was easy to centrifuge it out of the solution even at low speed of 4500 rpm rather than needing high speed centrifuge or other specialized techniques such as dialysis etc. Leaching studies were also undertaken to see the change in morphology and structure for the samples after dye adsorption and removal of dye. The data (Supplementry data Section: S3) clearly revealed that the dye adsorption on the sample GS1 is completely reversible and does not cause any structural and morphological changes in the sample.

The adsorption isotherms for MB and EBT dyes (Fig. 13 a and Fig. 13b) are obtained for change in % adsorption with change in dye (adsorbate) concentrations (1–50 mg/L) for a constant amount of adsorbent (100 mg/L). The increase in the dye amount increases the mass transfer force resulting in a higher amount of dye absorbed onto the surface of GS1 sample [55]. It is clear from Fig. 13a that the amount of MB adsorbed has a maximum value q
e
~52 mg/g at Ce ~20 mg/L. A further increase in the dye concentration (50 mg/L) results in a slight decrease which may be due to stearic hindrance offered by already adsorbed dye to further adsorption on the surface. For the EBT since the overall adsorption is less so no saturation is observed. The GS1 sample shows q
e
~31.5 mg/g of EBT for Ce ~50 mg/L.

Adsorption isotherms help with the adsorbent materials efficiency interpretation and the theoretical model fits to the data provide information about the nature of the adsorption [54]. Two adsorption isotherm models, namely Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models have been fitted to the experimental data obtained for adsorption of MB and EBT dyes onto GS1 sample. Fig. S5 and Fig. S6 give the graphs for isotherm fitting for MB and EBT dye, respectively.

The experimentally calculated values of amount of dye adsorbed at time t, q
t (mg/g) and amount of dye adsorbed at equilibrium, qe (mg/g), for MB and EBT adsorbed at any time t onto GS1 sample are obtained using the formulae [56].

Where, C0, C

Langmuir isotherm is applicable in case of monolayer adsorption of adsorbate onto adsorbent surface. The experimentally obtained data for MB and EBT dyes was fitted with the equation for Langmuir isotherm, expressed as [57]:

Four possible cases can occur: RL = 0 (irreversible adsorption), 0<RL < 1 (favorable), RL = 1 (linear) and RL > 1 (unfavorable) [58].

kL and qe for MB and EBT are determined from the linear fit of plots in Fig. S5a and Fig. S6a (Table 4 ). The value of RL for MB and EBT is 0.8 and 0.98, respectively, indicating favorable adsorption for both the dyes. The values of regression coefficient for MB and EBT are 0.981 and 0.0049, respectively. The very small regression coefficient value for EBT dye adsorption data clearly indicates the non adherence to Langmuir model adsorption model for this dye.

Freundlich isotherm model represents a multilayer adsorption. The linear form of equation for this isotherm is [57]:
(v)

where, kf represents the Freundlich constant (L/g) and n is the Freundlich exponent (g/L). The experimentally obtained data for MB and EBT dyes were fitted with Freundlich isotherm equation and are shown in Fig. S5b and Fig. S6b, respectively. The parameters obtained from the Freundlich fitting are given in Table 4. The value of n between 1 and 10 indicates that adsorption processes are favorable. The values of n obtained are 2.330 and 1.087 for MB and EBT, respectively, indicating that the adsorption is favorable in both cases. The values of regression coefficients for MB and EBT are 0.9591 and 0.9095, respectively.

The comparison of regression coefficient values for both the models discerns that the MB dye adsorbs onto the GS1 sample by monolayer formation [59]. The emergence of Freundlich model as a better fit for EBT indicated that it adsorbs on the surface of GS1 sample by forming multilayers [57].

To determine the rate and mechanism of adsorption process, it is important to study the adsorption kinetics. The kinetic data for the adsorption of cationic dye (MB) and anionic dye (EBT) onto GS1 sample was tested for well known kinetic models, namely pseudo first order (PFO) model and pseudo second order (PSO) model and Elovich model. To study the diffusion mechanism involved in the adsorption, the data were fitted with intraparticle diffusion (IPD) model. Fitting of models was done in the experiments in which 1 mg/L of the respective dye was used as adsorbate and 20 mg/L of GS1 sample was used as adsorbent. The experimental obtained values of qe using equation (ii) are 25.70 and 3.07 mg/g for MB and EBT, respectively.

PFO assumes that the amount of adsorption occurs is directly proportional to no. of available sites. The equation for PFO can be expressed as [60]:
(vi)

q e

Here, k1 is the rate constant (min−1) for PFO. Details of the experimental data fitted with PFO model are shown in Fig. S7a and Fig. S8a for MB and EBT, respectively. The parameters obtained from the PFO fitting are given in Table 5 . The values of % Δqe (the difference between experimental and theoretically calculated values of qe) for MB and EBT are 9.6% and 1.2% respectively.

This model is applied for the analysis of adsorption equilibrium capacity and adsorption occurs by means of chemisorption. The equation involved in PSO model can be presented as [61]:

The rate constant k2 (g/mg min.) and equilibrium adsorbate amount on the adsorbent qe (mg/g) were determined using slope and intercept of the linear fit of graph between t/q
t and time t. Fig. S7b and Fig. S8b shows the fitting of experimental data with PSO model for MB and EBT dye, respectively and the parameters obtained are mentioned in Table 5 along with regression coefficients. The values of % Δqe for MB and EBT are 25.8% and 25.7%, respectively.

Elovich model assumes that the adsorbent is of heterogeneous nature. This model is applicable when the multilayer adsorption process is taking place through chemisorptions. The fitting of experimental data with Elovich model is shown in Fig. S7c and Fig. S8c for MB and EBT dye, respectively following the equation [61]:

The kinetic parameters, initial adsorption rate α (mg/g min.) and desorption constant β, were determined using slope and intercept of straight line fit and are given in Table 5.

Based on the values of % Δqe and regression coefficients, it emerged that the adsorption of EBT dye follows PFO adsorption kinetics more favorably than PSO and Elovich adsorption kinetics [58]. In case of MB dye although considering %Δqe values the PFO model seems to be in better agreement with the experimental data among the PFO and PSO model. But the value of regression coefficient indicates that Elovich model is more consistent for MB dye i.e. the adsorption is taking place via chemisorption.

To determine the rate limiting step in adsorption process, IPD model is generally applied. Fig. S7d and Fig. S8d give the fitting of experimental data with IPD model for MB and EBT dye, respectively and follows the equation [58]:
(ix)
q t
=
k id
t
+ C
The rate constants kid1 (mg/g min1/2) and kid2 (mg/g min1/2) were determined using slopes of the multiple linear fits. The parameters obtained from fitting of IPD model are given in Table 6 .

For both the dyes the regression coefficient values are high (>0.99) for the IPD fits to the adsorption data. So, it can be concluded that in both the cases, adsorption is taking place by intrapaticle diffusion [62]. The linear fits pass through origin indicating that the mechanism is completely IPD controlled for both MB and EBT [63].

Initially, for both MB and EBT the adsorption onto the GS1 sample is fast and saturates to final value at a later stage (>150 min) as shown in Fig. 10a. Table 7 lists the results obtained from different model fittings to various isotherm and kinetics data.

For MB: Langmuir nature of the isotherm indicated that for MB the reversible adsorption at multiple equivalent sites leading to monolayer formation on the surface of GS1 takes place. The monolayer nature of the adsorption is also confirmed by the saturation of the % adsorption with the increase in dye concentration in the solution (Fig. 13a). Elovich kinetic fit indicated that the MB adsorbs strongly on to GS1 via chemisorption. This in accordance with the opposite charge of the dye and GS1 at the relevant pH (section 3.3.2). 2 step fit of IPD indicated that initially adsorption takes place through boundary layer diffusion and then by intraparticle diffusion beyond ~91 min [58].

For EBT: Freundlich nature of the isotherm indicated that for EBT GS1 is a heterogeneous adsorbent with the sites of N-doping (Lewis-Acid sites) behaving as favorable adsorption sites. This information in conjunction with the PFO kinetics model emerging as the most favorable model clearly indicated that adsorption occurs by multilayer physisorption. The multilayer nature of the adsorption is also confirmed by the non-saturation of the % adsorption with the increase in dye concentration in the solution (Fig. 13b). Two step fit of IPD indicated that initially adsorption takes place through boundary layer diffusion and then by intraparticle diffusion beyond ~109 min [58].

The value of kinetics rate constants is higher for MB as compared with those for EBT. This is because MB is a basic dye and the adsorbent is acidic (pHpzc = 4.6) so the MB dye uptake on the adsorbent is faster than that of anionic EBT. EBT contain acidic functional group on its surface and hence the adsorption is slower. This difference in interaction mechanism of the two dyes is also clear from the rate constants values of the two dyes with kid1 value of EBT (0.153) is an order of magnitude smaller than that for MB (1.687). After diffusive mass transfer, intrapaticle diffusion occur in both the cases as shown by second linear curve. The rate constants for interaparticle diffusion are higher compared with that for the initial boundary layer diffusion indicating the enhancement of adsorption rates for both the dyes after the initial boundary layer diffusion.

The comparison of adsorption of MB and EBT onto GS1 with the other popular adsorbents has been shown in Table 8 . It can be seen that despite the low temperature synthesis process and cheap green materials/agrowaste, the synthesized adsorbent ( GS1 ) shows significant adsorption capacity in comparison with the others.
Mixing of all the 3 dyes results in enhancement of the adsorption capacity (Supplementry data section: S5).

It has been reported that carbon nanospheres show photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds [34,36]. To test the GS1 sample for photocatalytic activity the %decolorization for MB under dark, UV light, visible light and sunlight irradiation was tested and the results are shown in Fig. 14 a. The data clearly shows that the %decolorization of MB dye gets enhanced under sunlight, UV and visible radiations in comparison with that for dark. The enhancement was maximum for sunlight because it contains mixture of UV and visible light with higher intensity. The enhancement for UV light was in agreement with the band gap of 4.5 eV obtained from UV–Visible spectroscopy study. The further photocatalytic studies has been undertaken only for UV light (λ < 400 nm) irradiation. Fig. 14b compares the % decolorization of MB dye for GS1 , S and G samples. GS1 sample showed better % decolorization as compared with the other two. We believe one of the underlying reason for it can be the better adsorption characteristics shown by GS1 sample (Fig. 9a). To test the recyclability of the sample under UV-light, it was tested for 5 consecutive cycles for MB % decolorization under UV light irradiation. The sample shows similar % decolorization in all cases without any decrement (Fig. 14c). Hence, the sample is completely recyclable for use under UV light.

Fig. 15 shows the comparison of the UV–Vis absorbance spectra for dark and UV light irradiation after 3 hours for all the 3 dyes. Clear shifting of locations of the UV–Vis absorbance spectra peaks for CV and EBT shows the emergence of the degradation byproducts under UV light irradiation [73]. Table 9 compares the % decolorization after 3 hours for dark and UV irradiated samples for the three dyes (MB, CV and EBT). MO shows zero adsorption at the natural pH so it was not tested under UV-light irradiation conditions. Table 9 clearly shows that the enhancement in % decolorization was small (20%) for MB so decolorization essentially proceeds via adsorption coupled with small amount of photodegradation. There is no noticeable shifting observed in UV–Vis absorbance spectra for MB dye [73]. In the case of CV and EBT, especially the latter the change is more dramatic, hence these dyes are being degraded more effectively. There is red shift in the absorbance maxima: ≈20 nm for CV and ≈70 nm for EBT, showing the degradation of dye after 3 hours (Fig. 15b and c). The possible reason for the stark contrast in the behavior of the MB and other dyes can be explained by the isotherm fitting data for the two systems. The Langmuir model fit for MB dye adsorption indicated strong interaction so the release is difficult. Whereas, for EBT the interaction is less so the molecules adsorb, get degraded and are easily removed from the surface for more adsorption. These results show that the synthesized samples are capable of removing the dyes from aqueous solution both by adsorption as well as photocatalytic activity under UV irradiation.

The effect of concentration of the sample on % decolorization under UV light was also studied for MB dye. From Fig. 15d, it can be noted that with the increase in the sample concentration from 4 mg/L to 20 mg/L, % decolorization increases from 65 to 71.5% due to increased availability of surface sites. The %degradation of MB in the absence of adsorbent under UV-light irradiation is about 3%, as shown in Fig. S11.

Decolorization of MB and EBT were also tested under sunlight. The % decolorization of MB and EBT with time is shown in Fig. 16 . Fig. 14a compares the % decolorization of MB dye under dark, UV, Visible and Solar light irradiation. The maximum % decolorization was observed for sunlight as given in Table 9 . This may be attributed to the higher intensity of sun light (~17500 lux) in comparison with UV lamp as well as wider wavelength spectrum of solar light.

N-doped high yield carbon spheroids were synthesized using agrowaste (soy flour) assisted by glucose via the simple HTC method. The chemical characterization (XPS) of the samples shows that the nitrogen content in the final samples can be tailored based on the glucose:soy flour ratio in the initial precursor. The large size of synthesized samples makes them easier to handle. The yield obtained for the GS1 sample is ≈ 2 and 45 folds more than that for S and G samples, respectively. The synthesis mechanism of spheroids has been explained on the basis of systematic structural, morphological and chemical chracterizations (XRD, SEM, HR-TEM, UV–Vis). It emerges that the nucleation of the spheroids is started by the derivatives of the glucose forming CQDs. These CQDs are successively encapsulated by the N-doped graphene/graphene oxide platelets. The nitrogen and oxygen in the constituent platelets (formed from soy protein) ensures that the final structures formed are expanded/large in morphology. To the best of our knowledge this is the first work in which the carbonization and spheroid formation mechanism for protein rich systems in the presence of the sachharides has been explained completely.

The predominantly pyrrolic nature of N-doping ensures that the synthesized samples are relevant for both cationic and anionic dye adsorption and are recyclable. The pHpzc of GS1 sample is 4.6 in comparison to 3.6 for S sample making it more favorable for anionic as well as cationic dye adsorption. The adsorption tests were done for two cationic (MB and CV) and two anionic (MO and EBT) dyes. The % adsorption of MB, CV and EBT are 51.5, 50, 6.1 in 3 hours. Change of pH dramatically changed the adsorption characteristics with higher pH being more favorable for cationic dyes and lower pH for anionic dyes. For MB and EBT the maximum equilibrium adsorption values are q
e
~52 mg/g and q
e
~31.5 mg/g respectively. The adsorption isotherms and kinetics models were fitted for MB and EBT dyes. The MB dye is adsorbed by fast chemisorption leading to the formation of monolayers on the surface of GS1. The EBT in contrast adsorbs via formation of multilayers by physisorption with low valued rate constants. When compared with the other relevant adsorbents the sample synthesized in this work shows significant adsorption capacity in comparison with the others. Comparing the availability and low cost of the precursors, followed by low temperature single step synthesis (no further surface area enhancements), high yield and ease of handling makes this material a very attractive candidate for dye removal adsorbents for industrial applications. Further, the synthesized sample also shows photocatalytic dye degradation under UV, Visible and solar light irradiation. This aspect of the synthesized samples opens new avenues and needs to be studied further to arrive at the mechanism of degradation.
